items.title: Key symbol upsert on a stable SourceLocusKey
items.description: Persist symbols through a convergent upsert keyed on a SourceLocusKey that identifies a declaration by its logical location (project, file, qualified name, signature) rather than by a generated id. Re-extracting the same source converges on the same row, so a symbol's Uid is stable across runs and downstream links do not break.
items.applicability: Every symbol-store write triggered by import, re-import, or re-extraction of a file.
items.example: A method re-extracted after an unrelated edit elsewhere keeps its original Uid because its SourceLocusKey is unchanged, preserving any experience or knowledge links pointing at it.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: build the SourceLocusKey from stable locators and upsert by it inside a transaction. Test implication: extract, re-extract, and assert the Uid is identical and no duplicate row appears. Generator implication: generated references to a symbol Uid remain valid across regenerations. Boundary: moving a declaration to a new file or renaming it changes the locus key, which correctly creates a new identity rather than tracking the move as the same symbol.

items.title: Detect change with a locus-independent content digest
items.description: Compute a change-detection digest over a symbol's meaningful content that is independent of its file location, so an identical re-extraction is a verified no-op. If the digest matches the stored value, skip the write entirely; only a changed digest triggers an update.
items.applicability: Re-extraction pipelines that must avoid needless writes and churn on unchanged code.
items.example: Re-importing the same ZIP produces matching digests for every unchanged symbol, so the store performs zero updates and emits a no-change status.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: hash normalized content (excluding volatile file-position data) and compare before writing. Test implication: re-extract unchanged input and assert no rows are updated. Generator implication: stable digests let the generator detect which symbols actually changed between runs. Boundary: the digest must exclude locus so a moved-but-identical symbol is recognised as unchanged content; conversely a whitespace-only edit that survives normalization will not register as a change.

items.title: Resolve parent links in a second pass after ids exist
items.description: Assign symbol ids in a first pass, then resolve parent/child links in a second pass once every symbol has an id. A declaration cannot reliably link to its parent during the first pass because the parent may not yet be persisted; deferring linkage avoids forward-reference gaps and dangling parents.
items.applicability: Building any hierarchical structure (namespace > type > method) where children and parents are discovered in arbitrary order.
items.example: A nested type's parent link is set in the second pass after both the outer and inner types have ids, regardless of which was parsed first.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: separate id assignment from link resolution into two explicit phases. Test implication: parse children before parents in a fixture and assert all parent links resolve. Generator implication: hierarchy queries are reliable because no parent is ever null when one exists. Boundary: a parent that genuinely does not exist in the corpus stays unlinked rather than being fabricated.

items.title: Capture C# references by simple name without a semantic model
items.description: Without a Compilation or semantic model, capture C# references by the simple identifier as written in source, not a fully-qualified name. Store them as unresolved candidates to be matched against known symbols in a later confidence-scored pass, never asserting a precise binding from syntax alone.
items.applicability: Reference extraction for any corpus that may not compile or whose dependencies are not imported.
items.example: A reference to 'OrderService' is captured as the token 'OrderService' and later matched, with confidence, against any symbol of that simple name in scope.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store the raw token plus context and defer matching to a resolution pass. Test implication: assert a reference to a type captured by simple name with no FQN and no semantic model. Generator implication: the generator treats unresolved references as hints weighted by confidence. Boundary: simple names are ambiguous across namespaces, so resolution can yield multiple candidates and must not silently pick one as definitive.

items.title: Score reference resolution with explicit confidence
items.description: When matching a captured simple-name reference to candidate symbols, attach an explicit confidence reflecting how uniquely the name resolves — a single in-scope candidate scores high, multiple candidates score lower. Consumers branch on confidence rather than assuming every resolution is certain.
items.applicability: The resolution pass that converts captured reference tokens into edges between symbols.
items.example: A unique 'PaymentService' resolves at high confidence; an ambiguous 'Helper' present in three namespaces resolves to all three at reduced confidence.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute confidence from candidate count and scope proximity and persist it on the edge. Test implication: assert unique and ambiguous names receive distinct confidence values. Generator implication: risky edits prefer high-confidence references; reporting may surface low-confidence ones for human review. Boundary: confidence is a heuristic ordering, not a probability, and cannot recover the certainty a semantic model would give.

items.title: Bridge C# to SQL by scanning string literals best-effort
items.description: Detect C#-to-SQL relationships by scanning string literals for SQL object names (tables, procedures, views) and linking the containing method to the matching schema symbol. This is best-effort and confidence-scored because it relies on textual heuristics over literals, not parsed SQL.
items.applicability: Connecting application code to database schema in estates that issue SQL through string queries or stored-procedure names.
items.example: A method whose literal contains 'EXEC dbo.PostPayment' links to the PostPayment procedure symbol at a confidence reflecting the heuristic match.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: scan literals for known schema names and emit confidence-scored bridge edges. Test implication: assert a literal naming a real procedure produces a bridge edge and an unrelated literal does not. Generator implication: impact analysis can cross the code/SQL boundary where the bridge fires. Boundary: dynamically built SQL, aliased names, and names assembled from fragments will be missed; the bridge is a hint, not a guarantee of data access.

items.title: Compute cyclomatic complexity syntactically
items.description: Derive a syntactic cyclomatic complexity for each method as one plus the count of decision points (branches, loops, conditional operators, catch clauses, and boolean short-circuits) found in the syntax tree. No semantic model is required; the count is purely structural.
items.applicability: Flagging methods that are risky to change and prioritising tests and reviews.
items.example: A method with two if statements and one loop reports complexity 4 (1 + 3 decision points), surfacing it as a higher-risk node.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: count decision-point syntax kinds in a single tree walk. Test implication: assert known fixtures produce the expected counts including the +1 base. Generator implication: complexity weights a node in impact and review prioritisation. Boundary: syntactic counting does not measure semantic difficulty, dead branches, or runtime path feasibility; it is a structural proxy only.

items.title: Route extraction through a language-pluggable router
items.description: Dispatch each file to a language-specific extractor through a router that selects by language (C#, Python, T-SQL) and returns an Empty result for unknown languages. The router is the single extension point; adding a language means registering an extractor, not editing call sites.
items.applicability: Mixed-language estates where files of several languages flow through one ingestion pipeline.
items.example: A .cs file routes to the C# extractor, a .py file to the Python extractor, a .sql file to the T-SQL extractor, and a .txt file returns Empty without error.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: register extractors against language keys and have the router return a well-defined Empty for unmatched inputs. Test implication: assert each language routes correctly and an unknown extension returns Empty, not an exception. Generator implication: new language support is additive and isolated. Boundary: the router only selects an extractor; the quality of extraction is bounded by each language extractor's own capabilities.

items.title: Return a deterministic extraction status, never a silent zero
items.description: Every extraction reports an explicit status — Extracted when symbols were produced, NoSymbols when the file legitimately has none — so a result of zero is always distinguishable from a failure or an unsupported file. The pipeline never silently records a bare zero count.
items.applicability: Ingestion observability and trust: distinguishing an empty file from a broken extractor.
items.example: An interface-only file reports NoSymbols deliberately, while a parse failure reports an error status, so dashboards do not conflate the two.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: model status as an explicit enum returned alongside the symbol list. Test implication: assert an empty-but-valid file yields NoSymbols and a parse failure yields an error status, never a plain zero. Generator implication: the generator can trust that zero symbols means a real decision was made. Boundary: status describes the extraction outcome, not whether the symbols found are semantically complete.

items.title: Insert nodes idempotently
items.description: Node insertion into the graph or store must be idempotent: inserting a node that already exists returns the existing node rather than creating a duplicate. Idempotent insertion is what lets re-extraction and incremental builds run safely without accumulating phantom vertices.
items.applicability: All graph and store writes during initial build, incremental update, and re-extraction.
items.example: Building the graph twice over the same corpus yields the same node count because the second pass finds and reuses every existing node.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: look up by stable key before inserting and return the existing node on a hit. Test implication: insert the same node twice and assert one node and one id. Generator implication: repeated generation runs do not bloat the graph. Boundary: idempotency depends on a correct stable key; a flawed key would let near-duplicates slip through, so key quality must be tested too.

items.title: Never create a dangling edge
items.description: An edge may only be created when both endpoints exist as nodes. If a target cannot be resolved to a real or external node, either materialise the missing endpoint deliberately (for example an external placeholder) or drop the edge — never leave an edge pointing at a non-existent node.
items.applicability: Every edge-producing step: containment, references, inheritance, entity and DbSet usage, and test coverage.
items.example: An inheritance edge to a framework base is given an external node first; a reference that resolves to nothing is dropped rather than left dangling.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate both endpoints before adding an edge and choose materialise-or-drop explicitly. Test implication: assert no edge in a built graph references a missing node. Generator implication: traversals never hit a null endpoint, so impact walks are safe. Boundary: dropping unresolved edges loses information about references to code outside the corpus unless they are deliberately materialised as external nodes.

items.title: Preserve downstream links across re-extraction
items.description: Because symbol Uids are stable under convergent upsert, links from experiences, knowledge items, and tests to a symbol survive re-extraction. Treat this stability as a guarantee downstream features may rely on, and protect it with tests so a future change to the locus key does not silently orphan those links.
items.applicability: Any subsystem that stores references to symbol Uids — experience memory, knowledge mapping, test coverage.
items.example: An experience linked to a method's Uid still resolves after the file is re-imported, because the Uid did not change.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep the locus key derivation stable and version any change to it deliberately. Test implication: link an experience to a symbol, re-extract, and assert the link still resolves. Generator implication: accumulated project memory is durable across re-imports. Boundary: a deliberate, versioned change to the key (or a genuine rename/move) will reassign identity, and dependent links must be migrated, not assumed permanent.

items.title: Defer cross-file matching to a global resolution pass
items.description: Resolve references across files only after every file has been extracted and every symbol persisted, so the resolution pass sees the complete symbol set. Per-file resolution would miss targets defined in files processed later and produce inconsistent edges.
items.applicability: Multi-file corpora where a reference in one file commonly targets a declaration in another.
items.example: A controller referencing a service defined in a different file resolves correctly because matching runs after both files are in the store.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: stage extraction and resolution as distinct phases over the whole corpus. Test implication: place a target in a later-processed file and assert the reference still resolves. Generator implication: cross-file impact is complete for the imported corpus. Boundary: references to symbols outside the imported corpus still cannot resolve and remain candidates or external links.

items.title: Keep extraction deterministic and order-independent
items.description: Extraction output must be deterministic and independent of file-processing order: the same corpus yields the same symbols, edges, digests, and statuses on every run. Determinism is what makes change-detection digests, idempotent inserts, and benchmark counts trustworthy.
items.applicability: The whole symbol and graph pipeline, especially where parallelism could introduce ordering effects.
items.example: Extracting the corpus in two different file orders produces identical node and edge counts and identical digests.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: sort or key any order-sensitive collection and avoid embedding wall-clock or random data in identity. Test implication: shuffle input order and assert identical output. Generator implication: regenerations and benchmarks are reproducible. Boundary: determinism covers extraction output; it does not constrain any downstream model whose output may still vary.

items.title: Capture data access with UsesEntity and UsesDbSet edges
items.description: Model data access explicitly with UsesEntity edges (a method referencing a mapped entity type) and UsesDbSet edges (a method touching a DbSet property on a context). Distinguishing the two lets reasoning separate which code reads a domain type from which code goes through the persistence context, both inferred syntactically.
items.applicability: Tracing data flow and impact from application code to entities and the database in EF-based estates.
items.example: A service method referencing an Order entity gets a UsesEntity edge, and the same method accessing context.Orders gets a UsesDbSet edge, so a schema change can find both the entity users and the query sites.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: detect entity references and DbSet member access during the syntax walk and emit the matching edge type. Test implication: assert a method that reads a DbSet produces UsesDbSet and one that references the entity produces UsesEntity. Generator implication: impact analysis for a schema change traverses these edges to the affected code. Boundary: syntactic detection misses data access through indirection (a repository abstraction or a reflected query), so these edges are a lower bound on real data dependency.
